investigationSummary.firstPassError: The first-pass delta file (plugin-agent-1-skills-deltas-docs-commit-8410e1c.json) concluded hasDeltas=false on the grounds that field-level discrepancies 'predated this commit'. That reasoning was wrong. Pre-commit data-models.mdx only contained TypeScript/JS resolver request types (commentAnnotation, metadata, event, ResolverActions). This commit introduced the first formally authoritative Python-specific field-level specs (organizationId, documentId, commentAnnotations, annotationId, commentId: int, etc.). Therefore, this commit is the event that makes existing rule field names verifiably wrong.
typesNewlyDocumented: VeltSelfHostingResponse (Python dict envelope — newly canonical spec)
VeltApiResponse (Python dict envelope — newly canonical spec)
DeleteCommentResolverRequest (Python fields: organizationId, documentId, annotationId, commentId: int)
DeleteAttachmentResolverRequest (Python fields: organizationId, documentId, attachmentId — no commentId)
DeleteReactionResolverRequest (Python fields: organizationId, documentId, reactionId — no commentId)
SaveAttachmentResolverRequest (Python fields: organizationId, documentId, attachmentId, metadata)

deltas.releaseNoteText: SaveCommentResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.comments.saveComments. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes), commentAnnotations (list[dict], Yes) — Annotation/comment data to upsert.
deltas.summary: The required payload field for SaveCommentResolverRequest is 'commentAnnotations' (list[dict]), not 'comments'. The rule's example passes comments=[...] which is a wrong field name — velt-py will silently ignore it or raise an error.

parameters.name: comment_annotations
parameters.description: Annotation/comment data to upsert. Python snake_case of the documented commentAnnotations field.

deltas.releaseNoteText: DeleteCommentResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.comments.deleteComment. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes), annotationId (str, Yes) — Annotation containing the comment, commentId (int, Yes) — ID of the comment to delete.
deltas.summary: DeleteCommentResolverRequest requires both 'annotationId' (which annotation the comment belongs to) and 'commentId' (int). The rule's example omits annotation_id entirely, making every delete call fail.

parameters.name: annotation_id
parameters.description: ID of the annotation containing the comment to delete

parameters.name: comment_id
parameters.description: Integer ID of the comment to delete

details.codeExample:
from velt_py import DeleteCommentResolverRequest

request = DeleteCommentResolverRequest(
    organization_id="org_123",
    document_id="doc_456",
    annotation_id="annotation_1",
    comment_id=1
)

response = sdk.selfHosting.comments.deleteComment(request)

deltas.releaseNoteText: DeleteAttachmentResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.attachments.deleteAttachment. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes), attachmentId (str, Yes) — ID of the attachment to delete.
deltas.summary: DeleteAttachmentResolverRequest has no 'comment_id' field — the spec requires only organizationId, documentId, and attachmentId. The rule incorrectly passes comment_id='comment_1' which is a spurious field not in the spec.

parameters.name: attachment_id
parameters.description: ID of the attachment to delete

details.codeExample:
from velt_py import DeleteAttachmentResolverRequest

request = DeleteAttachmentResolverRequest(
    organization_id="org_123",
    document_id="doc_456",
    attachment_id="attachment_789"
)

response = sdk.selfHosting.attachments.deleteAttachment(request)

deltas.releaseNoteText: DeleteReactionResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.reactions.deleteReaction. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes), reactionId (str, Yes) — ID of the reaction to delete.
deltas.summary: DeleteReactionResolverRequest has no 'comment_id' field — the spec requires only organizationId, documentId, and reactionId. The rule incorrectly passes comment_id='comment_1' alongside reaction_id.

parameters.name: reaction_id
parameters.description: ID of the reaction to delete

details.codeExample:
from velt_py import DeleteReactionResolverRequest

request = DeleteReactionResolverRequest(
    organization_id="org_123",
    document_id="doc_456",
    reaction_id="reaction_1"
)

response = sdk.selfHosting.reactions.deleteReaction(request)

deltas.releaseNoteText: GetReactionResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.reactions.getReactions. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes).
deltas.summary: GetReactionResolverRequest requires only organizationId and documentId. The rule also passes comment_id='comment_1' which is not in the spec. Remove comment_id from the example.

details.codeExample:
from velt_py import GetReactionResolverRequest

request = GetReactionResolverRequest(
    organization_id="org_123",
    document_id="doc_456"
)

response = sdk.selfHosting.reactions.getReactions(request)

deltas.releaseNoteText: SaveReactionResolverRequest — Request payload for sdk.selfHosting.reactions.saveReactions. Fields: organizationId (str, Yes), documentId (str, Yes), reactions (list[dict], Yes) — Reaction objects to upsert.
deltas.summary: SaveReactionResolverRequest requires only organizationId, documentId, and reactions. The rule passes comment_id='comment_1' which is not in the spec. Remove comment_id from the example.

deltas.releaseNoteText: VeltSelfHostingResponse — Standard envelope for all sdk.selfHosting.* methods. Success: { 'success': True, 'statusCode': 200, 'data': { ... } }. Error: { 'success': False, 'statusCode': 500, 'error': '...', 'errorCode': 'INTERNAL_ERROR' }. python.mdx framework examples use result.get('statusCode', 200) confirming dict-style access.
deltas.summary: The canonical VeltSelfHostingResponse is a plain Python dict with camelCase keys (statusCode, errorCode), not a Python object with snake_case attributes. Rules teaching response.error_code and response.status_code as attribute access will produce AttributeError at runtime if the SDK returns a plain dict. The docs and framework examples consistently use dict-style access.

details.signature: { 'success': bool, 'statusCode': int, 'data': dict | None, 'error': str | None, 'errorCode': str | None }
details.codeExample:
response = sdk.selfHosting.comments.getComments(request)

# Correct: dict-style access with camelCase keys
if response['success']:
    comments = response['data']
else:
    print(f"Error {response['errorCode']}: {response['error']}")

# Also valid for optional field access
status = response.get('statusCode', 200)

deltas.ambiguityNote: The docs show VeltSelfHostingResponse as a plain dict with camelCase keys (statusCode, errorCode). python.mdx framework examples consistently use result.get('statusCode', 200) — dict access. However, it is possible that velt-py wraps the response in a dataclass with snake_case attribute access (response.status_code, response.error_code), and the docs only show the wire-format JSON shape. The existing rules (written from velt-py 0.1.9 SDK observation) use attribute-style access. This delta should be confirmed against the actual velt-py library return type before applying.
